issues.title: PresenceBar shows no real team member data and has no construction role context
issues.description: PresenceBar reads from Liveblocks useOthers but never enriches presence data with construction roles (superintendent, PM, subcontractor), trade, or company name. Avatars show initials only with no role badge. In construction, knowing WHO is viewing a drawing (architect vs sub) is critical for coordination. The component also lacks any fallback when Liveblocks context is unavailable beyond silently rendering nothing.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/collaboration/PresenceBar.tsx, in the PresenceBarContent component and the getPresenceStatus function area, make these specific changes: BEFORE: The avatar tooltips show only user name and a relative time like 'just now' or '5 min ago'. There is no role, company, or trade information displayed. When Liveblocks is unavailable, the error boundary renders null with no user feedback. AFTER: (1) Extend the tooltip content to show a second line with the user's role and company if available from the presence metadata (e.g. 'Project Manager, Turner GC'). Add a small colored dot next to the name indicating their role category: blue for GC team, purple for architect/engineer, orange for owner, gray for subcontractor, matching the BIC color coding from the RFI page spec. (2) When the error boundary catches, instead of rendering null, render a small muted text span saying 'Presence unavailable' with a RefreshCw icon button that calls `this.setState({ hasError: false })` to retry. (3) Add an aria-live='polite' region that announces when a new user joins: 'UserName joined as Role'. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: EditConflictGuard lacks construction workflow integration and missing two-step verification support
issues.description: The optimistic lock checks updated_at but has no awareness of construction-specific status transitions (e.g. daily log submitted cannot be edited, punch item in 'sub complete' awaiting GC verification). The conflict dialog shows generic 'someone edited this' without explaining WHAT changed or WHO changed it, which is critical for legal defensibility of construction documents.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/collaboration/EditConflictGuard.tsx, in the useOptimisticLock hook and any conflict dialog rendering, make these specific changes: BEFORE: The hook only compares updated_at timestamps generically. The conflict toast says 'Could not verify if others have edited this item. Your changes may overwrite recent updates.' with no details about who edited or what changed. There is no awareness of entity status locks (submitted daily logs, verified punch items). AFTER: (1) Add an optional `lockedStatuses` parameter to useOptimisticLock (type: string[]) that lists statuses which prevent editing (e.g. ['submitted', 'approved', 'verified']). When the server query returns, also fetch the `status` field. If status is in lockedStatuses, set a new `isStatusLocked` boolean to true and store the locked status value. (2) Enhance the conflict warning toast to include the server's updated_at formatted as a readable date/time: 'This item was updated at 3:45 PM today. Your changes may conflict.' (3) Export the isStatusLocked boolean and lockedStatus string from the hook return so consuming components can show 'This daily log has been submitted and is locked for editing' or 'This punch item is awaiting GC verification' banners. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: Zero AI integration in collaboration module, no conflict prediction or smart notifications
issues.description: The collaboration module has no AI features. No smart conflict prediction ('Jennifer and Mike are both editing the same RFI'), no AI suggested resolution when conflicts occur, no intelligent notification routing based on role and current activity. Competitors like Procore are adding AI agents. SiteSync's collaboration needs AI to differentiate.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/collaboration/PresenceBar.tsx, in the PresenceBarContent component, make these specific changes: BEFORE: The presence bar shows who is online but provides no intelligence about potential conflicts or coordination opportunities. There is no AI insight panel or smart suggestion. AFTER: (1) Add a new section below the avatar row: a collapsible 'AI Insights' bar that appears only when there are 2+ users on the same entity type. Show a sparkle icon (from lucide-react, import Sparkles) followed by a brief text like 'Mike and Jennifer are both viewing RFIs. 3 RFIs have overlapping scope areas.' (2) This insight should be computed client-side from the presence store data: count users by entity type, and if 2+ users are on the same entity, show the coordination hint. Use theme colors (colors.accent for the sparkle, colors.textSecondary for the message text). (3) Make the insight dismissible with an X button that stores dismissal in sessionStorage keyed by entity type so it does not reappear for that session. Style with background colors.surface, border 1px solid colors.border, borderRadius borderRadius.md, padding spacing[2]. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: EntityPresence overflow dropdown not keyboard accessible and missing ARIA roles
issues.description: The overflow dropdown in PresenceDots opens on click but has no role='menu' on the dropdown, no role='menuitem' on items, no Escape key handler to close it, and no aria-expanded on the trigger button. The individual avatar buttons have minWidth/minHeight 44px but the inner dot is only 24px with no visible focus ring. Screen readers cannot navigate the presence indicators meaningfully.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/collaboration/EntityPresence.tsx, in the PresenceDots component, make these specific changes: BEFORE: The overflow toggle button has onKeyDown handler for Enter/Space but the dropdown div has no role attribute. Items inside the dropdown are plain divs with no role='menuitem'. There is no Escape key handling to close the dropdown. The trigger button lacks aria-expanded and aria-haspopup attributes. Individual avatar buttons have no visible focus ring style. AFTER: (1) Add aria-haspopup='true' and aria-expanded={overflowOpen} to the overflow toggle button. (2) Add role='menu' to the dropdown container div and role='menuitem' tabIndex={0} to each overflow viewer div inside it. (3) Add an onKeyDown handler on the dropdown container: if Escape is pressed, close dropdown and return focus to the toggle button via overflowToggleRef.current?.focus(). If ArrowDown is pressed on the last item, wrap to first. If ArrowUp on first, wrap to last. (4) Add a visible focus ring to all avatar buttons: outline: `2px solid ${colors.primary}`, outlineOffset: '2px' on focus-visible (use onFocus/onBlur state or CSS-in-JS approach with a focused state). (5) Add aria-label to the overflow toggle: `${overflow} more viewer${overflow !== 1 ? 's' : ''}`. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: No meeting collaboration features, no real-time meeting minutes or action item tracking
issues.description: The Meetings page (per PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA.md section 13) requires real-time collaborative note-taking, action item generation during meetings, and carry-forward of unresolved items. Currently the collaboration module provides presence and edit locks but has no meeting-specific collaboration: no shared notepad, no live attendee check-in, no action item extraction. This is a P0 gap for enterprise GCs who run weekly OAC meetings.
issues.prompt: In the file src/pages/Meetings.tsx, make these specific changes: BEFORE: The Meetings page shows a list of meetings but has no real-time collaborative editing of meeting minutes, no live attendee check-in, and no action item tracking with carry-forward. Meeting detail view (if it exists) is static. AFTER: (1) Add a 'Meeting Notes' section to the meeting detail view (or create one if only list view exists). This section should contain a textarea with a label 'Meeting Minutes' that auto-saves to the meeting record's `minutes` field via supabase every 3 seconds (debounced). Show a 'Saving...' / 'Saved' indicator next to the textarea. (2) Add an 'Action Items' section below the notes: a simple list with an 'Add Action Item' button. Each action item has: description (text input), assignee (dropdown from project directory), due date (date input), status (open/completed checkbox). Store in meeting_action_items table if it exists, or as a JSONB array on the meeting record. (3) Add a 'Check In' button for attendees that records their attendance timestamp. Show a list of expected attendees with green checkmarks for checked-in and gray circles for not yet arrived. (4) Use theme tokens for all styling. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

invented_features.title: Smart Coordination Alerts
invented_features.description: When two users from different trades (electrician and plumber) are both viewing drawings in the same building area simultaneously, surface an AI alert: 'Electrical and plumbing teams are both planning work in Mechanical Room 203 this week. Review coordination drawing A-M201 for potential conflicts.' This prevents the #1 cause of field rework: trades working in the same space without coordination.

invented_features.title: Edit History Diff Viewer for Legal Defensibility
invented_features.description: Construction documents (daily logs, RFIs, change orders) are legally discoverable. Add a 'View Change History' button that shows a side-by-side diff of every edit with user, timestamp, and before/after values. This satisfies SOC 2 audit trail requirements and protects GCs in litigation. No competitor offers this level of edit transparency.

competitive_intel: Procore has basic presence indicators (who is viewing a document) but no real-time collaborative editing. Their new Agent Builder adds AI but not to collaboration specifically. Fieldwire has no collaboration features beyond task assignment. Autodesk BIM 360 has real-time markup collaboration on 3D models but not on 2D drawings or documents. SiteSync's edit conflict guard with optimistic locking is a strong foundation, but without construction-role-aware presence, meeting collaboration, and AI conflict prediction, it falls short of the vision. The biggest gap is meeting minutes collaboration, which is a daily workflow for every PM running OAC meetings.
summary: The Collaboration module has solid infrastructure (presence via Liveblocks, optimistic locking, edit conflict detection) but scores low because it lacks construction domain integration, AI intelligence, and enterprise features. Presence shows who is online but not their role, trade, or company, which is critical context in construction. Edit conflict detection is generic and unaware of construction status locks (submitted logs, verified punch items). There is zero AI integration, no meeting collaboration, and accessibility gaps in the overflow dropdown. The five issues target: enriching presence with construction roles, adding status-aware edit locks, introducing AI coordination insights, fixing keyboard accessibility, and building real-time meeting collaboration. These changes would move the module from a generic collaboration toolkit to a construction-specific coordination platform.
